item.title: Ronan Farrow’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The journalist and author praises the perfectly calibrated tension in NOTORIOUS, talks about Jane Fonda’s dynamic performance in KLUTE, and shares what makes FANNY AND ALEXANDER such a personally resonant film.

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews Nobody 2
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
Suburban dad Hutch Mansell, a former lethal assassin, is pulled back into his violent past after thwarting a home invasion, setting off a chain of events that unravels secrets about his wife Becca's past and his own.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
Years into their relationship, Tim and Millie find themselves at a crossroads as they move to the country. With tensions already flaring, an encounter with an unnatural force threatens to corrupt their lives, their love and their flesh.

item.title: When an Actor Doesn’t Need Hollywood – Mads Mikkelsen
item.author: FilmStack
item.content:encoded:
Step confidently into daily life with the waterproof, ultra-light Vessi Sneakers. Covered with a 1-year warranty and 30-day worry-free returns. Get 15% off your first pair at http://vessi.com/filmstack at checkout!
Mads Mikkelsen is not your typical movie star. He didn’t start acting until his 30s with Pusher, The Green Butchers, and After the Wedding, but didn’t break out in Hollywood until he was 40.
While most actors would’ve cashed in on the success of Casino Royale, instead he turned down roles that didn’t interest him and chose the ones that pushed him further like Valhalla Rising, A Royal Affair, and The Hunt. It wasn’t about building a brand, it was about chasing the next great part.
But when you’re that good, Hollywood doesn’t let you stay away for long.
Over time, Mikkelsen became a go-to actor for everything: Marvel (Doctor Strange), Star Wars (Rogue One), Indiana Jones, Fantastic Beasts. He led Hannibal, gave one of the best video game performances in Death Stranding, and still always returned to Danish cinema, almost like Hollywood was funding the roles he was most passionate about like Another Round, Riders of Justice and The Promised Land.
This is the story of an actor that was too cool for Hollywood. Someone who dipped in and out of the system and still became a fan favourite. This is Mads Mikkelsen.
Chapters: 00:00 - Intro 00:52 - Better Late Than Never 06:33 - Not a Full Leap 11:40 - Perfectly Balanced 17:02 - One Role at a Time
Mads Mikkelsen. Hannibal. Death Stranding. Casino Royale. Le Chiffre. Flickering Lights. Open Hearts. The Green Butchers. Adam's Apples. After the Wedding. Valhalla Rising. Clash of the Titans. A Royal Affair. The Hunt. The Salvation. Men & Chicken. Doctor Strange. Rogue One Star Wars. Arctic. At Eternity's Gate. Another Round. Riders of Justice. Fantastic Beasts: The Secrets of Dumbledore. Indiana Jones and the Dial of Destiny. The Promised Land. Mufasa. Denmark. Danish Actor. Best Foreign Film.
If you like this video don't forget to leave a like, and if you're interested in videos about movies and the film industry in general, make sure to subscribe to FilmStack for more great content.
If you have any other ideas for videos, leave a comment and I might make a video with your idea.
Patreon: https://patreon.com/FilmStack Twitter: https://twitter.com/realFilmStack Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/realfilmstack/
#madsmikkelsen #actor #deathstranding

item.title: TRACY LETTS - Actor / Writer
item.author: jamesdeakins@me.com (jamesdeakins@me.com)
item.content:encoded: SEASON 2 - EPISODE 154 - Tracy Letts - Actor / WriterIn this episode of the Team Deakins Podcast, we speak with actor and writer Tracy Letts (AUGUST: OSAGE COUNTY, BUG, HOMELAND). Born and raised in Oklahoma, Tracy struck out for Chicago after high school to find work as an actor in the city’s active theatre scene, and we learn why Tracy began writing plays himself. We also discuss cinema’s role as a messenger for exposing the stories of the stage to rural American communities without access to the theatre, and he shares his mixed experiences adapting his plays into films, from director William Friedkin’s tense vision of BUG to the Hollywood ensemble version of his Pulitzer Prize winning play AUGUST: OSAGE COUNTY. An accomplished actor as well, Tracy reflects on seeking advice for performing for the camera from his seasoned HOMELAND castmates Claire Danes and Mandy Patinkin, and he reveals how he felt when, after being replaced on a film he had written, he was asked to act in additional scenes invented for his character. Plus, Tracy quotes a critical review that ironically sold out the remaining shows of his first play KILLER JOE. This episode is sponsored by Barco & Aputure

item.title: Jamie Lee Curtis's Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The actor shares how THIS IS SPINAL TAP changed her life, praises the transportive quality of films like DANTON and MONSOON WEDDING, and talks about her personal connection to SWEET SMELL OF SUCCESS and THE MANCHURIAN CANDIDATE.

item.title: Half in the Bag: Weapons
item.author: RedLetterMedia
item.content:encoded: What the heck is a Naruto run? I keep seeing comments that the kids in Weapons do the "Naruto run" when they leave their houses. Is this some kind of anime crap? Keep those filthy anime comparisons out of our comments section. We don't need that pervert filth around here. If you leave a comment about a Naruto run, we're going to email you a computer virus. Anyway speaking of pervert filth, we're here today to talk about the new film from the director of Barbarian!

item.title: RZA’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The rapper, record producer, composer, and filmmaker talks about how the ONCE UPON A TIME IN CHINA series elevated the martial-arts genre, praises MIKEY AND NICKY as a master class in how to write a buddy movie, and shouts out his longtime collaborator Jim Jarmusch.

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews Freakier Friday
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
22 years after Tess and Anna endured an identity crisis, Anna now has a daughter and a soon-to-be stepdaughter. As they navigate the challenges that come when two families merge, Tess and Anna discover that lightning might strike twice.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews Inside
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
Transferred from juvenile to adult prison, Mel is taken under the wing of both Shepard, Australia's most despised criminal, and Warren, a soon-to-be-paroled inmate. The paternal triangle that forms between them becomes their undoing.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

item.title: The Criterion Mobile Closet is Coming to the Toronto International Film Festival!
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded:
After wildly successful stops in multiple cities across the US, we’re making our first stop outside the country at the Toronto International Film Festival from September 4 - 7!
Find us on King St. across from the TIFF Lightbox — come in, explore the collection, and make your own Closet video! Sign up and receive updates and get all the important information you'll need to visit us: https://bit.ly/4fx4Gce

item.title: STEVEN SODERBERGH - Director
item.author: jamesdeakins@me.com (jamesdeakins@me.com)
item.content:encoded: SEASON 2 - EPISODE 153 - Steven Soderbergh - DirectorIn this extended episode of the Team Deakins Podcast, we speak with director Steven Soderbergh (BLACK BAG, TRAFFIC, OUT OF SIGHT). Steven credits his father for giving him the movie bug, and he shares how a young adulthood shooting short films and writing spec scripts in Louisiana led to the 8-years-in-the-making overnight success of his debut feature: SEX, LIES, AND VIDEOTAPE. We spend much of the episode discussing Steven’s insights into directing and filmmaking, and he opens up about his transition into shooting his own films under the alias Peter Andrews. Steven also reflects on directing OUT OF SIGHT, recalling how he ended up with the job, when a studio note protected him from his own instinct to be lean in the film’s edit, and why he felt the movie was at stake when he went to war to cast Jennifer Lopez. Steven also stresses the importance of character and reputation in the business, and he recalls the pitfalls of acting like an egomaniac that he witnessed while crewing in his early days. We also discuss the value of establishing a basic grammar and set of rules specific to the film one is making, and Steven shares how he tries to listen to what a movie wants “itself” to be.Recommended Viewing: OUT OF SIGHT This episode is sponsored by Aputure & Barco

item.title: Ari Aster’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The filmmaker returns to the Criterion Closet, where he praises seventies thrillers like THE PARALLAX VIEW and STRAW DOGS, talks about YOU CAN COUNT ON ME and how “no one writes dialogue like Kenneth Lonergan,” and connects TARGETS to his new film EDDINGTON.

item.title: Danny Boyle’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The filmmaker cites ALL THAT JAZZ as a major influence on modern editing, reveals the connection between MEMORIES OF UNDERDEVELOPMENT and TRAINSPOTTING, and shares his theory on why a director’s first film is always their best.

item.title: Mark Kermode reviews The Legend of Ochi
item.author: Kermode and Mayo's Take
item.content:encoded:
#MarkKermode #SimonMayo #FilmReviews
In a remote village on the island of Carpathia, a shy girl is raised to fear an elusive animal species known as ochi. But when she discovers a wounded baby ochi has been left behind, she escapes on a quest to bring him home.
To hear more, follow Kermode and Mayo's Take wherever you get your podcasts: https://podfollow.com/1616559297

item.title: Why The Movies Will Never Feel The Same Again
item.author: Thomas Flight

item.title: LUCY PREBBLE - Playwright / Writer
item.author: jamesdeakins@me.com (jamesdeakins@me.com)
item.content:encoded: SEASON 2 - EPISODE 152 - Lucy Prebble - Playwright / WriterPlaywright and writer Lucy Prebble (SUCCESSION, I HATE SUZIE, ENRON) joins us in this episode of the Team Deakins Podcast. Lucy’s plays frequently incorporate the physical space of the stage in the telling of the story and involve the audience in engaging and creative ways, and we were excited to talk to her about storytelling both on stage and on screen. We learn how, after a bookish childhood, she eventually found the courage to have her own plays put on, and she reflects on her experiences running her own television show and working on others’. Lucy also wrote for the amply-budgeted video game DESTINY, and she shares (as much as she’s allowed to) how the writing process works for something with that many resources. Lucy also reveals the real-world events that inspired her plays ENRON and THE SUGAR SYNDROME, and we learn how she identifies the real human relationships at the heart these stories. We also discuss the value of empathy and deadlines for a writer, and Lucy makes a prediction for the future of the theatre in the digital age. Plus, we observe that many of history’s greatest writers were really just frustrated actors.This episode is sponsored by Barco & Aputure

item.title: Barbie Ferreira’s Closet Picks
item.author: CRITERION
item.content:encoded: The actor selects favorite horror films like VIDEODROME and THE VANISHING, talks about connecting with the work of Gregg Araki, and recommends that everyone watch Todd Haynes’s SAFE.

item.title: Street Trash - re:View
item.author: RedLetterMedia
item.content:encoded: What's this? Jay is talking about another filthy garbage movie? What else is new? This time however, he is joined by Josh Robert Thompson, the world's foremost Street Trash expert. Together, they have the most thorough, thoughtful discussion of the underseen cult movie that anyone has ever had. Buckle up, prudes!
